title: Getting started with HubbellGLM: the Barro Colorado Island dataset
text: HubbellGLM package implements Hubbell regression, generalized linear model (GLM) α\alpha-diversity (Zito et al. 2026). model links environmental covariates Hubbell’s fundamental biodiversity number α\alpha, single dimension-free parameter controls rate new species accumulate function sample size, can converted analytically Shannon entropy, Simpson index, Hill numbers. bypassess need choose biodiversity index use analysis. vignette introduces statistical framework demonstrates package using Barro Colorado Island (BCI) dataset, classic tree-census dataset 50-ha tropical forest plot Panama. original version data downloaded R package vegan (Oksanen et al. 2024).

title: Getting started with HubbellGLM: the Barro Colorado Island dataset
text: Let Y(n)Y^{(n)} denote number distinct species (richness) observed sample nn individuals drawn community. Hubbell’s neutral theory biodiversity (Hubbell 2001), distribution Y(n)Y^{(n)} given ℙ(Y(n)=y;α)=αyΓ(α)Γ(α+n)|s(n,y)|,y=1,…,n, \mathbb{P}(Y^{(n)} = y;\, \alpha) = \frac{\alpha^y \,\Gamma(\alpha)}{\Gamma(\alpha + n)}\,|s(n, y)|, \qquad y = 1, \ldots, n, |s(n,y)||s(n, y)| (unsigned) Stirling numbers first kind, Γ(⋅)\Gamma(\cdot) gamma function, α>0\alpha > 0 fundamental biodiversity number (Hubbell 2001). distribution arises Ewens sampling formula (Ewens 1972), mathematically equivalent Dirichlet process described (Ferguson 1973) (Antoniak 1974). model, richness Y(n)Y^{(n)} accumulates logarithmically function nn, limn→∞Y(n)log⁡n=α. \lim_{n \\infty} \frac{Y^{(n)}}{\log n} = \alpha.  relationship makes α\alpha equivalent biodiveristy number log-series model proposed (Fisher et al. 1943). larger α\alpha means richer, even communities; smaller α\alpha means communities dominated abundant species. particular, expected species richness sample size nn μ=𝔼(Y(n))=∑j=0n−1αα+j. \mu = \mathbb{E}(Y^{(n)}) = \sum_{j=0}^{n-1} \frac{\alpha}{\alpha + j}. Hence, mean interpreted species accumulation curve grows logarithmically nn. Moreover, since Hubbell’s model equivalent Dirichlet process, standard biodiversity indices closed-form model-based expressions terms α\alpha(Rigon et al. 2025; Pitman 2006): ψ(⋅)\psi(\cdot) digamma function, B(⋅,⋅)B(\cdot,\cdot) beta function, pkp_k relative abundance kk-th species.

title: Getting started with HubbellGLM: the Barro Colorado Island dataset
text: Since distribution Y(n)Y^{(n)} exponential family, can use build generalized linear model. particular, goal link fundamental biodiversity number α\alpha 𝒙∈ℝp\mathbf{x} \\mathbb{R}^p. call model Hubbell regression. Specifically, letting =1,…,Ni = 1, \ldots, N denote sampling sites (.e. observations), let Yi(ni)∼Hubbell(αi,ni),log⁡αi=ηi=𝒙i⊤𝜷. Y_i^{(n_i)} \sim \text{Hubbell}(\alpha_i, n_i), \qquad \log \alpha_i = \eta_i = \mathbf{x}_i^\top \boldsymbol{\beta}. canonical link (σ=0\sigma = 0). one-unit increase covariate xipx_{ip} translates 100%(eβp−1)100\%(e^{\beta_p} - 1) change α\alpha-diversity.

title: Getting started with HubbellGLM: the Barro Colorado Island dataset
text: canonical link constrains species richness grow logarithmically nn. allow heavier-tailed abundance distributions (e.g. arthropods), package supports general polynomial link controlled σ<1\sigma < 1: μi=∑j=0ni−1eηieηi+j1−σ,σ<1. \mu_i = \sum_{j=0}^{n_i - 1} \frac{e^{\eta_i}}{e^{\eta_i} + j^{1-\sigma}}, \qquad \sigma < 1. σ=0\sigma = 0: logarithmic growth (canonical Hubbell link). σ∈(0,1)\sigma \(0, 1): polynomial growth; regression-based diversity index Sσ(η)=eη/σS_\sigma(\eta) = e^\eta / \sigma. σ<0\sigma < 0: finite asymptotic richness. parameter σ\sigma estimated maximum likelihood via estimate_sigma() held fixed across nested model specifications ensure comparable regression coefficients.

title: Getting started with HubbellGLM: the Barro Colorado Island dataset
text: Standard GLM inference assumes conditionally independent observations. species-richness data, species may appear multiple sites, inducing cross-site dependence likelihood ignores. HubbellGLM corrects using heteroskedastic-consistent sandwich standard errors, spatial dependence sites ii kk measured Jaccard similarity index siks_{ik} (fraction shared species two samples). Let 𝑿∈ℝN×p\mathbf{X} \\mathbb{R}^{N \times p} design matrix, 𝑾̂=diag(ŵ1,…,ŵN)\hat{\mathbf{W}} = \text{diag}(\hat{w}_1, \ldots, \hat{w}_N) matrix IRLS working weights, 𝒖̂=∂log⁡ℒ(𝜷)/∂𝜷|𝜷̂\hat{\mathbf{u}}_i = \partial \log \mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol{\beta}) / \partial \boldsymbol{\beta}|_{\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}} score observation ii. Jaccard-adjusted variance–covariance matrix sê(𝜷̂)=(𝑿⊤𝑾̂𝑿)−1(∑,ksik𝒖̂i𝒖̂k⊤)(𝑿⊤𝑾̂𝑿)−1. \widehat{\text{se}}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}) = (\mathbf{X}^\top \hat{\mathbf{W}} \mathbf{X})^{-1} \left(\sum_{,k} s_{ik}\, \hat{\mathbf{u}}_i \hat{\mathbf{u}}_k^\top \right) (\mathbf{X}^\top \hat{\mathbf{W}} \mathbf{X})^{-1}. computed vcov_species(fit, Dshared), Dshared N×NN \times N matrix pairwise Jaccard similarities. species membership sites unavailable (BCI example ), standard Fisher-information-based vcov(fit) used.

title: Getting started with HubbellGLM: the Barro Colorado Island dataset
text: main fitting function mirrors syntax stats::glm. response cbind(n, y), n sample size (number individuals) y observed richness. Key arguments: function returns object class HubbellGLM compatible summary(), coef(), vcov(), predict(), residuals(), deviance(), AIC(), BIC(), step().
code: fit <- HubbellGLM(cbind(n, y) ~ x1 + x2,                   family = hubbell(sigma = 0),                   data   = mydata)

title: Getting started with HubbellGLM: the Barro Colorado Island dataset
text: Sets GLM family. sigma = 0 gives canonical (logarithmic) link; value (−∞,1)(-\infty, 1) gives polynomial link. Use quasihubbell() quasi-likelihood inference variance suspected inflated.
code: hubbell(link = "polyseries", sigma = 0)

title: Getting started with HubbellGLM: the Barro Colorado Island dataset
text: Finds maximum-likelihood σ̂\hat\sigma minimising BIC null model σ∈(−∞,0.9)\sigma \(-\infty, 0.9) using nlminb. Fix σ\sigma value subsequent models ensure comparability regression coefficients across nested specifications.
code: sigma_hat <- estimate_sigma(cbind(n, y) ~ 1, data = mydata)

what: predict.HubbellGLM() — predict richness at new sites
title: Getting started with HubbellGLM: the Barro Colorado Island dataset
text: type = "link" returns η̂=𝒙⊤𝜷̂\hat\eta = \mathbf{x}^\top \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}. type = "response" returns predicted mean richness μ̂=g−1(η̂)\hat\mu = g^{-1}(\hat\eta).
code: predict(fit, newdata = new_sites, type = "response")

title: Getting started with HubbellGLM: the Barro Colorado Island dataset
text: Returns data frame columns n (sample size grid), pred (predicted richness), se (standard error via delta method). Useful visualising richness accumulates specific new location.
code: curve <- predict_curve(fit, xnew = new_site, n = 2000, npoints = 200)

title: Getting started with HubbellGLM: the Barro Colorado Island dataset
text: Returns sandwich variance–covariance matrix described Section 3. Pass lmtest::coeftest(fit, vcov = vcov_adj) obtain robust z-scores p-values.
code: Dshared  <- get_shared_species(species_matrix)  # N x N Jaccard matrix vcov_adj <- vcov_species(fit, Dshared)

title: Getting started with HubbellGLM: the Barro Colorado Island dataset
text: BarroColorado dataset contains 50 subplots Barro Colorado Island 50-ha plot. row one subplot : n: number individual trees sampled. y: number distinct species observed. EnvHet: environmental heterogeneity (continuous). Habitat: habitat type (factor 5 levels). Stream: proximity stream (Yes/). Observed species accumulation: richness vs. sample size across habitat types.
code: library(HubbellGLM)  data("BarroColorado") head(BarroColorado) #>     n   y EnvHet  Habitat Stream #> 1 448  93 0.6272 OldSlope    Yes #> 2 435  84 0.3936   OldLow    Yes #> 3 463  90 0.0000   OldLow     No #> 4 508  94 0.0000   OldLow     No #> 5 505 101 0.4608 OldSlope     No #> 6 412  85 0.0768   OldLow     No plot(BarroColorado$n, BarroColorado$y,      col  = as.integer(BarroColorado$Habitat),      pch  = 16, cex = 0.9,      xlab = "Sample size (n)",      ylab = "Observed richness (y)",      main = "BCI: richness vs. sample size") legend("bottomright", legend = levels(BarroColorado$Habitat),        col = 1:5, pch = 16, cex = 0.8)

title: Getting started with HubbellGLM: the Barro Colorado Island dataset
text: three models run canonical link
code: # M0: the null model fit_null <- HubbellGLM(cbind(n, y) ~ 1,                      family = hubbell(sigma = 0),                      data   = BarroColorado)  # M1: environmental heterogeneity only fit_M1 <- HubbellGLM(cbind(n, y) ~ EnvHet,                      family = hubbell(sigma = 0),                      data   = BarroColorado)  # M2: add habitat type fit_M2 <- HubbellGLM(cbind(n, y) ~ EnvHet + Habitat,                      family = hubbell(sigma = 0),                      data   = BarroColorado)  # M3: add stream proximity fit_M3 <- HubbellGLM(cbind(n, y) ~ EnvHet + Habitat + Stream,                      family = hubbell(sigma = 0),                      data   = BarroColorado)  summary(fit_M3) #>  #> Call: #> HubbellGLM(formula = cbind(n, y) ~ EnvHet + Habitat + Stream,  #>     family = hubbell(sigma = 0), data = BarroColorado) #>  #> Coefficients: #>                 Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)     #> (Intercept)      3.43326    0.05523  62.161  < 2e-16 *** #> EnvHet           0.07146    0.09230   0.774  0.43880     #> HabitatOldLow    0.14270    0.05534   2.579  0.00992 **  #> HabitatOldSlope  0.13352    0.06224   2.145  0.03193 *   #> HabitatSwamp     0.26655    0.11023   2.418  0.01560 *   #> HabitatYoung    -0.04071    0.10360  -0.393  0.69439     #> StreamYes       -0.10782    0.05879  -1.834  0.06665 .   #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 #>  #> (Dispersion parameter for hubbell family taken to be 1) #>  #>     Null deviance: 49.331  on 49  degrees of freedom #> Residual deviance: 34.543  on 43  degrees of freedom #> AIC: 343.26 #>  #> Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 3

code: round(100 * (exp(coef(fit_M3)) - 1), 2) #>     (Intercept)          EnvHet   HabitatOldLow HabitatOldSlope    HabitatSwamp  #>         2997.75            7.41           15.34           14.28           30.55  #>    HabitatYoung       StreamYes  #>           -3.99          -10.22

code:
cat("Null deviance:    ", round(deviance(fit_null), 1), "
") #> Null deviance:     49.3 cat("M1 deviance:      ", round(deviance(fit_M1),  1), "
") #> M1 deviance:       49.2 cat("M2 deviance:      ", round(deviance(fit_M2),  1), "
") #> M2 deviance:       37.9 cat("M3 deviance:      ", round(deviance(fit_M3),  1), "
") #> M3 deviance:       34.5 1 - deviance(fit_M3) / deviance(fit_null) #> [1] 0.2997722

title: Getting started with HubbellGLM: the Barro Colorado Island dataset
text: predict_curve() returns fitted accumulation curve (pointwise standard errors) new covariate combination. compare high-heterogeneity OldHigh subplot near-stream Young subplot. Predicted accumulation curves two contrasting subplot types.
code: new_sites <- data.frame(   n       = c(500, 500),   y       = c(1L,  1L),      # placeholder required by the formula   EnvHet  = c(0.6,  0.0),   Habitat = factor(c("OldHigh", "OldSlope"),                    levels = levels(BarroColorado$Habitat)),   Stream  = factor(c("No", "Yes"),                    levels = levels(BarroColorado$Stream)) )  curve1 <- predict_curve(fit_M3, xnew = new_sites[1, ], n = 600, npoints = 100) curve2 <- predict_curve(fit_M3, xnew = new_sites[2, ], n = 600, npoints = 100)  pred1 <- curve1$mean[, 1]; se1 <- curve1$se[, 1] pred2 <- curve2$mean[, 1]; se2 <- curve2$se[, 1]  plot(curve1$n, pred1, type = "l", col = "steelblue", lwd = 2,      ylim = range(c(pred1 - 2*se1, pred2 + 2*se2)),      xlab = "Sample size (n)", ylab = "Predicted richness",      main = "Predicted accumulation curves") lines(curve2$n, pred2, col = "tomato", lwd = 2)  # 95% CI bands polygon(c(curve1$n, rev(curve1$n)),         c(pred1 - 2*se1, rev(pred1 + 2*se1)),         col = adjustcolor("steelblue", 0.15), border = NA) polygon(c(curve2$n, rev(curve2$n)),         c(pred2 - 2*se2, rev(pred2 + 2*se2)),         col = adjustcolor("tomato", 0.15), border = NA)  legend("bottomright",        legend = c("OldHigh, high EnvHet, no stream",                   "Young, low EnvHet, near stream (OldSlope fixed)"),        col = c("steelblue", "tomato"), lwd = 2, cex = 0.8)

previous_headings: BCI example > The canonical link (logarithmic growth)
title: Getting started with HubbellGLM: the Barro Colorado Island dataset
text: α̂\hat\alpha recovered fitted model, standard biodiversity indices follow analytically.
code:
alpha_n <- function(mu, n) {   uniroot(function(a) mean_dirichlet_process(a, n) - mu,           c(1e-6, 1e6))$root }  # Predictions at n = 500 for each subplot mu_pred <- predict(fit_M3, type = "response") n_vec   <- BarroColorado$n  alpha_vals   <- mapply(alpha_n, mu_pred, n_vec) shannon_vals <- digamma(alpha_vals + 1) - digamma(1) simpson_vals <- 1 / (alpha_vals + 1)  cat("Median alpha:   ", round(median(alpha_vals),   3), "
") #> Median alpha:    35.729 cat("Median Shannon: ", round(median(shannon_vals), 3), "
") #> Median Shannon:  4.167 cat("Median Simpson: ", round(median(simpson_vals), 4), "
") #> Median Simpson:  0.0272

what: Model comparison via AIC and BIC
title: Getting started with HubbellGLM: the Barro Colorado Island dataset
text: Compare nested models directly using AIC() BIC(). model lowest AIC/BIC preferred. Note step.HubbellGLM() can also called directly automated backward/forward selection; see ?step.hubbell.
code: aic_vals <- c(   Null     = AIC(fit_null),   EnvHet   = AIC(fit_M1),   `+Habitat` = AIC(fit_M2),   `+Stream`  = AIC(fit_M3) ) bic_vals <- c(   Null     = BIC(fit_null),   EnvHet   = BIC(fit_M1),   `+Habitat` = BIC(fit_M2),   `+Stream`  = BIC(fit_M3) ) round(rbind(AIC = aic_vals, BIC = bic_vals), 2) #>       Null EnvHet +Habitat +Stream #> AIC 346.05 347.89   344.66  343.26 #> BIC 347.96 351.71   356.13  356.65

title: Getting started with HubbellGLM: the Barro Colorado Island dataset
text: fitting covariate model, estimate accumulation-curve growth rate null model. value near zero indicates near-logarithmic growth, consistent Dirichlet-process baseline. Values (0,1)(0, 1) indicate polynomial (heavier-tailed) growth, σ<0\sigma < 0 implies accumulation curve eventually converges finite number. —
code: sigma_hat <- estimate_sigma(formula = fit_M3$formula, data = BarroColorado) #> Evaluating sigma =  0  #> Evaluating sigma =  1.490116e-08  #> Evaluating sigma =  -1  #> Evaluating sigma =  -0.9999822  #> Evaluating sigma =  -0.9063331  #> Evaluating sigma =  -0.90634  #> Evaluating sigma =  -0.2472168  #> Evaluating sigma =  -0.7269597  #> Evaluating sigma =  -0.7269794  #> Evaluating sigma =  -0.5742515  #> Evaluating sigma =  -0.5742655  #> Evaluating sigma =  -0.6462702  #> Evaluating sigma =  -0.6462601  #> Evaluating sigma =  -0.6373282  #> Evaluating sigma =  -0.6373377  #> Evaluating sigma =  -0.6373282 cat("Estimated sigma:", round(sigma_hat, 4), "\n") #> Estimated sigma: -0.6373

code: @Manual{,   title = {HubbellGLM: Predicting biodiversity via the Hubbell regression},   author = {Alessandro Zito},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 1.0.0},   url = {https://alessandrozito.github.io/HubbellGLM/}, }

what: HubbellGLM: Covariate-dependent biodiversity via Hubbell regression
title: HubbellGLM: Covariate-dependent biodiversity via Hubbell regression
text: HubbellGLM implements Hubbell regression — generalized linear model (GLM) α\alpha-diversity. model treats observed species richness random variable governed Dirichlet process (.e. Ewens sampling formula) uses standard GLM machinery regress Hubbell’s fundamental biodiversity number α\alpha environmental covariates. single fitted model yields coefficients standard errors pp-values, likelihood-based model selection (AIC/BIC), closed-form predictions Shannon entropy, Simpson’s index, Hill numbers sample size.

title: HubbellGLM: Covariate-dependent biodiversity via Hubbell regression
text: Install development version GitHub:
code: # install.packages("devtools") devtools::install_github("alessandrozito/HubbellGLM")

title: HubbellGLM: Covariate-dependent biodiversity via Hubbell regression
code: #-- Load the R packages library(HubbellGLM) library(vegan) library(lmtest)  #-- Load the data data(BCI) data("BarroColorado")  # Estimate the growth-rate exponent sigma from the null model formula <- "cbind(n, y) ~ EnvHet + Habitat + Stream" sigma_hat <- estimate_sigma(formula, data = BarroColorado[1:40, ]) # -0.7852143 #sigma_hat <- -0.7852143  # Fit a covariate model fit <- HubbellGLM(formula,                   family = hubbell(sigma = sigma_hat),                   data   = BarroColorado[1:40, ]) summary(fit)  # Predict richness at new sites new_sites <- BarroColorado[41:50, ] predict(fit, newdata = new_sites, type = "response")  # Species accumulation curve with confidence band curve <- predict_curve(fit, xnew = new_sites, n = 600, npoints = 100) plot(curve$n, curve$mean[, 1] + 1.96 * curve$se[, 1], lty = "dashed", type = "l") lines(curve$n, curve$mean[, 1], col = "red") lines(curve$n, curve$mean[, 1] - 1.96 * curve$se[, 1], lty = "dashed")  # Optional: two ways of adjusting standard errors.   #=== Option 1 - Use the quasihubbell model fitQuasi <- HubbellGLM(formula,                   family = quasihubbell(sigma = sigma_hat),                   data   = BarroColorado[1:40, ]) summary(fitQuasi)  #=== Option 2 - Adjust standard errors via shared species (Jaccard similarity) brayDist <- vegdist(BCI[1:40, ]) Dshared <- 1 - as.matrix(2 * brayDist / (1 + brayDist)) vcov_fit <- vcov_shared(fit, similarity = Dshared) coeftest(fit, vcov. = vcov_fit)

title: HubbellGLM: Covariate-dependent biodiversity via Hubbell regression
text: Zito, ., Rigon, T., Roslin, T., Niittynen, P., Hebert, P. D. N., Zakharov, E. V., Ratnasingham, S., iBOL Consortium, Ovaskainen, O., Dunson, D. B. (2026). Predicting global biodiversity via Hubbell regression. bioArxiv.

EnvHet Environmental heterogeneity index (continuous, higher values     indicate greater habitat complexity). Habitat Habitat type (factor 5 levels:     OldHigh, OldLow, OldSlope, Swamp,     Young). Stream Proximity stream ("Yes" / "").

what: Invert the mean of the Dirichlet process distribution — inv_mean_dirichlet_process
title: Invert the mean of the Dirichlet process distribution — inv_mean_dirichlet_process
text: Given target mean \(\mu\) community size \(n\), finds concentration parameter \(\alpha\) \(E[Y^{(n)}; \alpha] = \mu\) via uniroot.

code: inv_mean_dirichlet_process(mu_target, size)

title: Invert the mean of the Dirichlet process distribution — inv_mean_dirichlet_process
text: mu_target Target mean value (scalar vector, must \((1, n)\)). size Community size (positive integer, scalar vector).

title: Invert the mean of the Dirichlet process distribution — inv_mean_dirichlet_process
text: concentration parameter \(\alpha\) (scalar vector).

title: Predict the accumulation curve from a HubbellGLM model for new observations. — predict_curve
code: predict_curve(fit, xnew, n = 3000, npoints = 100, .vcov = NULL)

title: Predict the accumulation curve from a HubbellGLM model for new observations. — predict_curve
text: fit object class HubbellGLM. xnew data.frame predictor columns used fit fit. row produces one set accumulation curve predictions. n Total number individuals extrapolate . Must single positive integer. npoints Number equally-spaced grid points 1 n. Must satisfy npoints <= n. Default 100. .vcov Optional variance-covariance matrix regression coefficients. Must square matrix dimension equal number coefficients fit. Defaults vcov(fit).

title: Predict the accumulation curve from a HubbellGLM model for new observations. — predict_curve
text: named list three elements: n Integer vector length npoints grid     points (number individuals). mean data.frame npoints rows one     column per row xnew, containing predicted species     richness grid point. se data.frame shape mean,     containing standard errors computed via delta method.

title: Stepwise model selection for HubbellGLM models — step.HubbellGLM
text: object fitted HubbellGLM object. scope formula list formulas defining range models consider. See step details. scale Scaling factor AIC penalty. Default 0. direction Direction stepwise search: "", "backward", "forward". trace Integer; positive, prints information step. keep function retain extra information step. steps Maximum number steps. Default 1000. k Penalty multiplier number parameters (2 AIC). ... Additional arguments passed fitting function.
